headline: Five Decades of Protection: Nepal's Sagarmatha Park at 50
meta_description: Fifty years after Nepal established Sagarmatha National Park around Mount Everest, the 1,148 sq km sanctuary protects snow leopards, 208 bird species, and 6,00…
summary_short: Half a century ago, Nepal created its first national park around Mount Everest, protecting one of Earth's most sacred and biodiverse mountain landscapes.
body:
On July 19, 1974, Nepal took a bold step toward conservation by establishing Sagarmatha National Park—the country's first protected area. Today, that decision has preserved one of the world's most remarkable ecosystems across 1,148 square kilometers of rugged Himalayan terrain.

The park encompasses everything from 2,845-meter gorges to the 8,848-meter summit of Mount Everest, known locally as Sagarmatha. What makes this protection extraordinary isn't just the iconic peak, but the living world it shelters: snow leopards, Asiatic leopards, musk deer, and 208 bird species including the bearded vulture and Impeyan pheasant. BirdLife International has recognized it as an Important Bird Area.

Eqally vital are the park's 6,000 Sherpas, who have called this region home for four centuries. Their traditional practices—restricting animal hunting and honoring all living beings—have shaped the landscape alongside its natural forces. Over 20 villages maintain these customs today.

The recognition expanded in 2002 when UNESCO inscribed Sagarmatha as a Natural Heritage Site. It now forms part of the Sacred Himalayan Landscape, a mosaic of protected areas shared with Tibet's Qomolangma National Nature Preserve and Makalu Barun National Park to the east.

Fifty years later, Sagarmatha National Park stands as a testament to what's possible when nations prioritize both nature and the communities that steward it.
